==Japanese==
'''東京十社'''（とうきょうじっしゃ）は、元准勅祭社12社の内から、[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）に定められた東京近郊の10の[[神社]]のことである。
== Overview == [[明治維新]]が成り[[東京奠都|東京に奠都]]した明治天皇は、[[1868]]（明治元年）[[10月17日 (旧暦)|10月17日]]、[[氷川神社]]の例大祭を新たに勅祭に定めた。それに続いて[[11月8日 (旧暦)|11月8日]]、それまでの[[畿内]][[二十二社]]などの[[勅祭社]]に併せて東京近郊の主だった神社を'''准勅祭社'''（じゅんちょくさいしゃ）と定めて[[東京]]の鎮護と万民の安泰を祈る神社とした。
当初は12社（[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]・[[根津神社]]・[[芝大神宮|芝神明宮]]・[[神田明神|神田神社]]・[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]・[[亀戸天神社|亀戸神社]]・[[品川神社]]・[[富岡八幡宮|富岡八幡神社]]・[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]・[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]・[[大國魂神社|六所神社]]・[[鷲宮神社]]）であった。しかし早くも[[1870]]（[[明治]]3年）[[9月1日 (旧暦)|9月1日]]には廃止され准勅祭社の制度は一時的なもので終わった。該当神社は府社あるいは郷社となる。第二次大戦後は政府による[[社格]]そのものが廃止された。なお、准勅祭社の品川神社については、<!-- 品川貴船社、現在の品川神社を稲荷神社としている例があること等から、この品川神社とは-->当時の品川貴船社、現在の[[荏原神社]]であるとする説もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=荏原神社-准勅祭社|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。<!--しかし品川神社第十三代神主小泉帯刀（准勅祭社指定当時の神主）の文書を根拠に現在の「東京十社めぐり」では品川神社説を採用している。-->現在「東京十社巡り」に荏原神社は入っていないが、品川神社、荏原神社ともに元准勅祭社を名乗っている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title= 品川神社【北の天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title= 荏原神社【天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。
[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）、[[昭和天皇]]即位50年を奉祝して関係神社が協議を行い、准勅祭社から遠隔の府中町六所宮と埼玉県久喜市の鷲宮神社を外し、23区内の10社を巡る「東京十社巡り」が企画され、[[七福神]]巡りなどとともに<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=東京十社巡りガイド|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>観光的な要素を濃くして現在に至っている。
== 東京十社一覧 == [[式内社]]に列せられた神社はない。「近代」は明治に成立した[[近代社格制度]]の社格、「別表」は戦後の[[別表神社]]を示す。社名・鎮座地は現在のもの。 {|class="wikitable" !社名!!通称・別名!!主祭神!!近代!!別表!!鎮座地(東京都)!!備考 |- |[[根津神社]]||根津権現||[[須佐之男命]]、[[大山咋命]]、[[誉田別命]]||[[府社]]||||[[文京区]][[根津]]|| |- |[[芝大神宮]]||芝神明宮、飯倉神明宮||[[天照大神]]、[[豊受大神]]||府社||||[[港区 (東京都)|港区]][[芝大門]]|| |- |[[神田明神|神田神社]]||神田明神||[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[平将門|平将門命]]||府社||[[別表神社|別表]]||[[千代田区]][[外神田]]||<!--<ref>新・東京五社（他に、日枝神社、東京大神宮、[[水天宮]]、[[金刀比羅宮 (東京都港区)|金刀比羅宮]]）の一つ。</ref>--> |- |[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]||山王権現||[[大山咋神]]||[[官幣大社]]||別表||千代田区[[永田町]]||<ref group="注釈">東京五社<!--（他に、[[明治神宮]]、[[靖国神社]]、[[大國魂神社]]、[[東京大神宮]]）-->の一つ<!--、新・東京五社の一つ-->。</ref> |- |[[亀戸天神社]]||東宰府、亀戸天満宮||[[菅原道真|天満大神（菅原道真）]]、[[アメノホヒ|天菩日命]]||府社||||[[江東区]][[亀戸]]|| |- |[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]||||[[菊理姫命]]、[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]||[[郷社]]||||文京区[[白山 (文京区)|白山]]|| |- |[[品川神社]]||北の天王、北品川稲荷社||天比理乃咩命、[[素盞嗚尊]]、[[トヨウケビメ|宇賀之売命]]||郷社||||[[品川区]][[北品川]]||<ref group="注釈">[[東海七福神]]の[[大黒天|大黒]]</ref> |-
|- |[[富岡八幡宮]]||深川八幡||[[応神天皇|八幡神（応神天皇）]]||府社||||江東区[[富岡 (江東区)|富岡]]||<ref group="注釈">[[深川七福神]]の恵比寿</ref><ref group="注釈">戦後、別表神社となったが、2017年に神社本庁を離脱した。</ref> |- |[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]||王子権現||伊弉諾命、伊弉冉命、天照大御神||郷社||||[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[王子本町]]|| |- |[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]||||素盞嗚尊、[[奇稲田姫命]]、大己貴命||府社||||港区[[赤坂 (東京都港区)|赤坂]]|| |}

当初は12社（[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]・[[根津神社]]・[[芝大神宮|芝神明宮]]・[[神田明神|神田神社]]・[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]・[[亀戸天神社|亀戸神社]]・[[品川神社]]・[[富岡八幡宮|富岡八幡神社]]・[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]・[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]・[[大國魂神社|六所神社]]・[[鷲宮神社]]）であった。しかし早くも[[1870]]（[[明治]]3年）[[9月1日 (旧暦)|9月1日]]には廃止され准勅祭社の制度は一時的なもので終わった。該当神社は府社あるいは郷社となる。第二次大戦後は政府による[[社格]]そのものが廃止された。なお、准勅祭社の品川神社については、<!-- 品川貴船社、現在の品川神社を稲荷神社としている例があること等から、この品川神社とは-->当時の品川貴船社、現在の[[荏原神社]]であるとする説もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=荏原神社-准勅祭社|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。<!--しかし品川神社第十三代神主小泉帯刀（准勅祭社指定当時の神主）の文書を根拠に現在の「東京十社めぐり」では品川神社説を採用している。-->現在「東京十社巡り」に荏原神社は入っていないが、品川神社、荏原神社ともに元准勅祭社を名乗っている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title= 品川神社【北の天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title= 荏原神社【天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。
[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）、[[昭和天皇]]即位50年を奉祝して関係神社が協議を行い、准勅祭社から遠隔の府中町六所宮と埼玉県久喜市の鷲宮神社を外し、23区内の10社を巡る「東京十社巡り」が企画され、[[七福神]]巡りなどとともに<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=東京十社巡りガイド|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>観光的な要素を濃くして現在に至っている。
== 東京十社一覧 == [[式内社]]に列せられた神社はない。「近代」は明治に成立した[[近代社格制度]]の社格、「別表」は戦後の[[別表神社]]を示す。社名・鎮座地は現在のもの。 {|class="wikitable" !社名!!通称・別名!!主祭神!!近代!!別表!!鎮座地(東京都)!!備考 |- |[[根津神社]]||根津権現||[[須佐之男命]]、[[大山咋命]]、[[誉田別命]]||[[府社]]||||[[文京区]][[根津]]|| |- |[[芝大神宮]]||芝神明宮、飯倉神明宮||[[天照大神]]、[[豊受大神]]||府社||||[[港区 (東京都)|港区]][[芝大門]]|| |- |[[神田明神|神田神社]]||神田明神||[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[平将門|平将門命]]||府社||[[別表神社|別表]]||[[千代田区]][[外神田]]||<!--<ref>新・東京五社（他に、日枝神社、東京大神宮、[[水天宮]]、[[金刀比羅宮 (東京都港区)|金刀比羅宮]]）の一つ。</ref>--> |- |[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]||山王権現||[[大山咋神]]||[[官幣大社]]||別表||千代田区[[永田町]]||<ref group="注釈">東京五社<!--（他に、[[明治神宮]]、[[靖国神社]]、[[大國魂神社]]、[[東京大神宮]]）-->の一つ<!--、新・東京五社の一つ-->。</ref> |- |[[亀戸天神社]]||東宰府、亀戸天満宮||[[菅原道真|天満大神（菅原道真）]]、[[アメノホヒ|天菩日命]]||府社||||[[江東区]][[亀戸]]|| |- |[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]||||[[菊理姫命]]、[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]||[[郷社]]||||文京区[[白山 (文京区)|白山]]|| |- |[[品川神社]]||北の天王、北品川稲荷社||天比理乃咩命、[[素盞嗚尊]]、[[トヨウケビメ|宇賀之売命]]||郷社||||[[品川区]][[北品川]]||<ref group="注釈">[[東海七福神]]の[[大黒天|大黒]]</ref> |-
|- |[[富岡八幡宮]]||深川八幡||[[応神天皇|八幡神（応神天皇）]]||府社||||江東区[[富岡 (江東区)|富岡]]||<ref group="注釈">[[深川七福神]]の恵比寿</ref><ref group="注釈">戦後、別表神社となったが、2017年に神社本庁を離脱した。</ref> |- |[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]||王子権現||伊弉諾命、伊弉冉命、天照大御神||郷社||||[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[王子本町]]|| |- |[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]||||素盞嗚尊、[[奇稲田姫命]]、大己貴命||府社||||港区[[赤坂 (東京都港区)|赤坂]]|| |}

==Japanese==
'''東京十社'''（とうきょうじっしゃ）は、元准勅祭社12社の内から、[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）に定められた東京近郊の10の[[神社]]のことである。
== Overview == [[明治維新]]が成り[[東京奠都|東京に奠都]]した明治天皇は、[[1868]]（明治元年）[[10月17日 (旧暦)|10月17日]]、[[氷川神社]]の例大祭を新たに勅祭に定めた。それに続いて[[11月8日 (旧暦)|11月8日]]、それまでの[[畿内]][[二十二社]]などの[[勅祭社]]に併せて東京近郊の主だった神社を'''准勅祭社'''（じゅんちょくさいしゃ）と定めて[[東京]]の鎮護と万民の安泰を祈る神社とした。
当初は12社（[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]・[[根津神社]]・[[芝大神宮|芝神明宮]]・[[神田明神|神田神社]]・[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]・[[亀戸天神社|亀戸神社]]・[[品川神社]]・[[富岡八幡宮|富岡八幡神社]]・[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]・[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]・[[大國魂神社|六所神社]]・[[鷲宮神社]]）であった。しかし早くも[[1870]]（[[明治]]3年）[[9月1日 (旧暦)|9月1日]]には廃止され准勅祭社の制度は一時的なもので終わった。該当神社は府社あるいは郷社となる。第二次大戦後は政府による[[社格]]そのものが廃止された。なお、准勅祭社の品川神社については、<!-- 品川貴船社、現在の品川神社を稲荷神社としている例があること等から、この品川神社とは-->当時の品川貴船社、現在の[[荏原神社]]であるとする説もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=荏原神社-准勅祭社|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。<!--しかし品川神社第十三代神主小泉帯刀（准勅祭社指定当時の神主）の文書を根拠に現在の「東京十社めぐり」では品川神社説を採用している。-->現在「東京十社巡り」に荏原神社は入っていないが、品川神社、荏原神社ともに元准勅祭社を名乗っている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title= 品川神社【北の天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title= 荏原神社【天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。
[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）、[[昭和天皇]]即位50年を奉祝して関係神社が協議を行い、准勅祭社から遠隔の府中町六所宮と埼玉県久喜市の鷲宮神社を外し、23区内の10社を巡る「東京十社巡り」が企画され、[[七福神]]巡りなどとともに<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=東京十社巡りガイド|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>観光的な要素を濃くして現在に至っている。
== 東京十社一覧 == [[式内社]]に列せられた神社はない。「近代」は明治に成立した[[近代社格制度]]の社格、「別表」は戦後の[[別表神社]]を示す。社名・鎮座地は現在のもの。 {|class="wikitable" !社名!!通称・別名!!主祭神!!近代!!別表!!鎮座地(東京都)!!備考 |- |[[根津神社]]||根津権現||[[須佐之男命]]、[[大山咋命]]、[[誉田別命]]||[[府社]]||||[[文京区]][[根津]]|| |- |[[芝大神宮]]||芝神明宮、飯倉神明宮||[[天照大神]]、[[豊受大神]]||府社||||[[港区 (東京都)|港区]][[芝大門]]|| |- |[[神田明神|神田神社]]||神田明神||[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[平将門|平将門命]]||府社||[[別表神社|別表]]||[[千代田区]][[外神田]]||<!--<ref>新・東京五社（他に、日枝神社、東京大神宮、[[水天宮]]、[[金刀比羅宮 (東京都港区)|金刀比羅宮]]）の一つ。</ref>--> |- |[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]||山王権現||[[大山咋神]]||[[官幣大社]]||別表||千代田区[[永田町]]||<ref group="注釈">東京五社<!--（他に、[[明治神宮]]、[[靖国神社]]、[[大國魂神社]]、[[東京大神宮]]）-->の一つ<!--、新・東京五社の一つ-->。</ref> |- |[[亀戸天神社]]||東宰府、亀戸天満宮||[[菅原道真|天満大神（菅原道真）]]、[[アメノホヒ|天菩日命]]||府社||||[[江東区]][[亀戸]]|| |- |[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]||||[[菊理姫命]]、[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]||[[郷社]]||||文京区[[白山 (文京区)|白山]]|| |- |[[品川神社]]||北の天王、北品川稲荷社||天比理乃咩命、[[素盞嗚尊]]、[[トヨウケビメ|宇賀之売命]]||郷社||||[[品川区]][[北品川]]||<ref group="注釈">[[東海七福神]]の[[大黒天|大黒]]</ref> |-
|- |[[富岡八幡宮]]||深川八幡||[[応神天皇|八幡神（応神天皇）]]||府社||||江東区[[富岡 (江東区)|富岡]]||<ref group="注釈">[[深川七福神]]の恵比寿</ref><ref group="注釈">戦後、別表神社となったが、2017年に神社本庁を離脱した。</ref> |- |[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]||王子権現||伊弉諾命、伊弉冉命、天照大御神||郷社||||[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[王子本町]]|| |- |[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]||||素盞嗚尊、[[奇稲田姫命]]、大己貴命||府社||||港区[[赤坂 (東京都港区)|赤坂]]|| |}

'''東京十社'''（とうきょうじっしゃ）は、元准勅祭社12社の内から、[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）に定められた東京近郊の10の[[神社]]のことである。
== Overview == [[明治維新]]が成り[[東京奠都|東京に奠都]]した明治天皇は、[[1868]]（明治元年）[[10月17日 (旧暦)|10月17日]]、[[氷川神社]]の例大祭を新たに勅祭に定めた。それに続いて[[11月8日 (旧暦)|11月8日]]、それまでの[[畿内]][[二十二社]]などの[[勅祭社]]に併せて東京近郊の主だった神社を'''准勅祭社'''（じゅんちょくさいしゃ）と定めて[[東京]]の鎮護と万民の安泰を祈る神社とした。
当初は12社（[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]・[[根津神社]]・[[芝大神宮|芝神明宮]]・[[神田明神|神田神社]]・[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]・[[亀戸天神社|亀戸神社]]・[[品川神社]]・[[富岡八幡宮|富岡八幡神社]]・[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]・[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]・[[大國魂神社|六所神社]]・[[鷲宮神社]]）であった。しかし早くも[[1870]]（[[明治]]3年）[[9月1日 (旧暦)|9月1日]]には廃止され准勅祭社の制度は一時的なもので終わった。該当神社は府社あるいは郷社となる。第二次大戦後は政府による[[社格]]そのものが廃止された。なお、准勅祭社の品川神社については、<!-- 品川貴船社、現在の品川神社を稲荷神社としている例があること等から、この品川神社とは-->当時の品川貴船社、現在の[[荏原神社]]であるとする説もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=荏原神社-准勅祭社|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。<!--しかし品川神社第十三代神主小泉帯刀（准勅祭社指定当時の神主）の文書を根拠に現在の「東京十社めぐり」では品川神社説を採用している。-->現在「東京十社巡り」に荏原神社は入っていないが、品川神社、荏原神社ともに元准勅祭社を名乗っている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title= 品川神社【北の天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title= 荏原神社【天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。
[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）、[[昭和天皇]]即位50年を奉祝して関係神社が協議を行い、准勅祭社から遠隔の府中町六所宮と埼玉県久喜市の鷲宮神社を外し、23区内の10社を巡る「東京十社巡り」が企画され、[[七福神]]巡りなどとともに<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=東京十社巡りガイド|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>観光的な要素を濃くして現在に至っている。
== 東京十社一覧 == [[式内社]]に列せられた神社はない。「近代」は明治に成立した[[近代社格制度]]の社格、「別表」は戦後の[[別表神社]]を示す。社名・鎮座地は現在のもの。 {|class="wikitable" !社名!!通称・別名!!主祭神!!近代!!別表!!鎮座地(東京都)!!備考 |- |[[根津神社]]||根津権現||[[須佐之男命]]、[[大山咋命]]、[[誉田別命]]||[[府社]]||||[[文京区]][[根津]]|| |- |[[芝大神宮]]||芝神明宮、飯倉神明宮||[[天照大神]]、[[豊受大神]]||府社||||[[港区 (東京都)|港区]][[芝大門]]|| |- |[[神田明神|神田神社]]||神田明神||[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[平将門|平将門命]]||府社||[[別表神社|別表]]||[[千代田区]][[外神田]]||<!--<ref>新・東京五社（他に、日枝神社、東京大神宮、[[水天宮]]、[[金刀比羅宮 (東京都港区)|金刀比羅宮]]）の一つ。</ref>--> |- |[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]||山王権現||[[大山咋神]]||[[官幣大社]]||別表||千代田区[[永田町]]||<ref group="注釈">東京五社<!--（他に、[[明治神宮]]、[[靖国神社]]、[[大國魂神社]]、[[東京大神宮]]）-->の一つ<!--、新・東京五社の一つ-->。</ref> |- |[[亀戸天神社]]||東宰府、亀戸天満宮||[[菅原道真|天満大神（菅原道真）]]、[[アメノホヒ|天菩日命]]||府社||||[[江東区]][[亀戸]]|| |- |[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]||||[[菊理姫命]]、[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]||[[郷社]]||||文京区[[白山 (文京区)|白山]]|| |- |[[品川神社]]||北の天王、北品川稲荷社||天比理乃咩命、[[素盞嗚尊]]、[[トヨウケビメ|宇賀之売命]]||郷社||||[[品川区]][[北品川]]||<ref group="注釈">[[東海七福神]]の[[大黒天|大黒]]</ref> |-
|- |[[富岡八幡宮]]||深川八幡||[[応神天皇|八幡神（応神天皇）]]||府社||||江東区[[富岡 (江東区)|富岡]]||<ref group="注釈">[[深川七福神]]の恵比寿</ref><ref group="注釈">戦後、別表神社となったが、2017年に神社本庁を離脱した。</ref> |- |[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]||王子権現||伊弉諾命、伊弉冉命、天照大御神||郷社||||[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[王子本町]]|| |- |[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]||||素盞嗚尊、[[奇稲田姫命]]、大己貴命||府社||||港区[[赤坂 (東京都港区)|赤坂]]|| |}

==Japanese==
'''東京十社'''（とうきょうじっしゃ）は、元准勅祭社12社の内から、[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）に定められた東京近郊の10の[[神社]]のことである。
== Overview == [[明治維新]]が成り[[東京奠都|東京に奠都]]した明治天皇は、[[1868]]（明治元年）[[10月17日 (旧暦)|10月17日]]、[[氷川神社]]の例大祭を新たに勅祭に定めた。それに続いて[[11月8日 (旧暦)|11月8日]]、それまでの[[畿内]][[二十二社]]などの[[勅祭社]]に併せて東京近郊の主だった神社を'''准勅祭社'''（じゅんちょくさいしゃ）と定めて[[東京]]の鎮護と万民の安泰を祈る神社とした。
当初は12社（[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]・[[根津神社]]・[[芝大神宮|芝神明宮]]・[[神田明神|神田神社]]・[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]・[[亀戸天神社|亀戸神社]]・[[品川神社]]・[[富岡八幡宮|富岡八幡神社]]・[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]・[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]・[[大國魂神社|六所神社]]・[[鷲宮神社]]）であった。しかし早くも[[1870]]（[[明治]]3年）[[9月1日 (旧暦)|9月1日]]には廃止され准勅祭社の制度は一時的なもので終わった。該当神社は府社あるいは郷社となる。第二次大戦後は政府による[[社格]]そのものが廃止された。なお、准勅祭社の品川神社については、<!-- 品川貴船社、現在の品川神社を稲荷神社としている例があること等から、この品川神社とは-->当時の品川貴船社、現在の[[荏原神社]]であるとする説もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=荏原神社-准勅祭社|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。<!--しかし品川神社第十三代神主小泉帯刀（准勅祭社指定当時の神主）の文書を根拠に現在の「東京十社めぐり」では品川神社説を採用している。-->現在「東京十社巡り」に荏原神社は入っていないが、品川神社、荏原神社ともに元准勅祭社を名乗っている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title= 品川神社【北の天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title= 荏原神社【天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。
[[1975]]（[[昭和]]50年）、[[昭和天皇]]即位50年を奉祝して関係神社が協議を行い、准勅祭社から遠隔の府中町六所宮と埼玉県久喜市の鷲宮神社を外し、23区内の10社を巡る「東京十社巡り」が企画され、[[七福神]]巡りなどとともに<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=東京十社巡りガイド|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>観光的な要素を濃くして現在に至っている。
== 東京十社一覧 == [[式内社]]に列せられた神社はない。「近代」は明治に成立した[[近代社格制度]]の社格、「別表」は戦後の[[別表神社]]を示す。社名・鎮座地は現在のもの。 {|class="wikitable" !社名!!通称・別名!!主祭神!!近代!!別表!!鎮座地(東京都)!!備考 |- |[[根津神社]]||根津権現||[[須佐之男命]]、[[大山咋命]]、[[誉田別命]]||[[府社]]||||[[文京区]][[根津]]|| |- |[[芝大神宮]]||芝神明宮、飯倉神明宮||[[天照大神]]、[[豊受大神]]||府社||||[[港区 (東京都)|港区]][[芝大門]]|| |- |[[神田明神|神田神社]]||神田明神||[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[平将門|平将門命]]||府社||[[別表神社|別表]]||[[千代田区]][[外神田]]||<!--<ref>新・東京五社（他に、日枝神社、東京大神宮、[[水天宮]]、[[金刀比羅宮 (東京都港区)|金刀比羅宮]]）の一つ。</ref>--> |- |[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]||山王権現||[[大山咋神]]||[[官幣大社]]||別表||千代田区[[永田町]]||<ref group="注釈">東京五社<!--（他に、[[明治神宮]]、[[靖国神社]]、[[大國魂神社]]、[[東京大神宮]]）-->の一つ<!--、新・東京五社の一つ-->。</ref> |- |[[亀戸天神社]]||東宰府、亀戸天満宮||[[菅原道真|天満大神（菅原道真）]]、[[アメノホヒ|天菩日命]]||府社||||[[江東区]][[亀戸]]|| |- |[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]||||[[菊理姫命]]、[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]||[[郷社]]||||文京区[[白山 (文京区)|白山]]|| |- |[[品川神社]]||北の天王、北品川稲荷社||天比理乃咩命、[[素盞嗚尊]]、[[トヨウケビメ|宇賀之売命]]||郷社||||[[品川区]][[北品川]]||<ref group="注釈">[[東海七福神]]の[[大黒天|大黒]]</ref> |-
|- |[[富岡八幡宮]]||深川八幡||[[応神天皇|八幡神（応神天皇）]]||府社||||江東区[[富岡 (江東区)|富岡]]||<ref group="注釈">[[深川七福神]]の恵比寿</ref><ref group="注釈">戦後、別表神社となったが、2017年に神社本庁を離脱した。</ref> |- |[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]||王子権現||伊弉諾命、伊弉冉命、天照大御神||郷社||||[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[王子本町]]|| |- |[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]||||素盞嗚尊、[[奇稲田姫命]]、大己貴命||府社||||港区[[赤坂 (東京都港区)|赤坂]]|| |}

{{draft}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|東京十社}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)|ja|氷川神社}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}, {{ill|Okunitama Shrine|ja|大國魂神社}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|ja|鷲宮神社}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different ranks. Shrine rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} registry. "Modern" indicates ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} after WWII.

{{draft}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|東京十社}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)|ja|氷川神社}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}, {{ill|Okunitama Shrine|ja|大國魂神社}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|ja|鷲宮神社}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different ranks. Shrine rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} registry. "Modern" indicates ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |{{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |{{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |{{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

{{draft}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|東京十社}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)|ja|氷川神社}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}, {{ill|Okunitama Shrine|ja|大國魂神社}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|ja|鷲宮神社}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different ranks. Shrine rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} registry. "Modern" indicates ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |{{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |{{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |{{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

{{draft}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|東京十社}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)|ja|氷川神社}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}, {{ill|Okunitama Shrine|ja|大國魂神社}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|ja|鷲宮神社}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different ranks. Shrine rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} registry. "Modern" indicates ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |{{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |{{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |{{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

{{draft}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|東京十社}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)|ja|氷川神社}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}, {{ill|Okunitama Shrine|ja|大國魂神社}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|ja|鷲宮神社}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different ranks. Shrine rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} registry. "Modern" indicates ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |{{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |{{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |{{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

{{draft}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|東京十社}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)|ja|氷川神社}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}, {{ill|Okunitama Shrine|ja|大國魂神社}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|ja|鷲宮神社}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different ranks. Shrine rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} registry. "Modern" indicates ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |{{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |{{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |{{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different ranks. Shrine rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} registry. "Modern" indicates ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |{{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |{{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |{{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

[[Category:Imported Shinto cults]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:東京十社]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:東京都区部の神社|*]] [[Category:Imported Shinto cults]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:東京十社]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:准勅祭社|*]] [[Category:Imported Shinto cults]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:東京十社]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines by tradition]] [[Category:Imported Shinto cults]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:東京十社]][[Category:kk]] [[Category: pages]] [[Category:東京十社]][[ja:東京十社]] [[Category:Imported Shinto cults]] [[Category:kk]][[ja:東京十社]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:日本の名数10]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:11]][[Category:東京十社]][[Category:11]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:ality]] [[Category:Https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[fr:Tokyo-jissha]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tokyo Ten Shrines]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokyo Ten Shrines]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 2]][[Category: tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tokyo Ten Shrines]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]]

{{draft}} {{ill|{{PAGENAME}}|ja|東京十社}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)|ja|氷川神社}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}, {{ill|Okunitama Shrine|ja|大國魂神社}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|ja|鷲宮神社}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different ranks. Shrine rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} registry. "Modern" indicates ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |{{ill|Nezu Shrine|ja|根津神社}}||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |{{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|ja|亀戸天神社}}||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |{{ill|Tomioka Hachimangu|ja|富岡八幡宮}}||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

revision.text:
'''東京十社'''（とうきょうじっしゃ）は、元准勅祭社12社の内から、[[1975年]]（[[昭和]]50年）に定められた東京近郊の10の[[神社]]のことである。
== 概要 == [[明治維新]]が成り[[東京奠都|東京に奠都]]した明治天皇は、[[1868年]]（明治元年）[[10月17日 (旧暦)|10月17日]]、[[氷川神社]]の例大祭を新たに勅祭に定めた。それに続いて[[11月8日 (旧暦)|11月8日]]、それまでの[[畿内]][[二十二社]]などの[[勅祭社]]に併せて東京近郊の主だった神社を'''准勅祭社'''（じゅんちょくさいしゃ）と定めて[[東京]]の鎮護と万民の安泰を祈る神社とした。
当初は12社（[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]・[[根津神社]]・[[芝大神宮|芝神明宮]]・[[神田明神|神田神社]]・[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]・[[亀戸天神社|亀戸神社]]・[[品川神社]]・[[富岡八幡宮|富岡八幡神社]]・[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]・[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]・[[大國魂神社|六所神社]]・[[鷲宮神社]]）であった。しかし早くも[[1870年]]（[[明治]]3年）[[9月1日 (旧暦)|9月1日]]には廃止され准勅祭社の制度は一時的なもので終わった。該当神社は府社あるいは郷社となる。第二次大戦後は政府による[[社格]]そのものが廃止された。なお、准勅祭社の品川神社については、<!-- 品川貴船社、現在の品川神社を稲荷神社としている例があること等から、この品川神社とは-->当時の品川貴船社、現在の[[荏原神社]]であるとする説もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=荏原神社-准勅祭社|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。<!--しかし品川神社第十三代神主小泉帯刀（准勅祭社指定当時の神主）の文書を根拠に現在の「東京十社めぐり」では品川神社説を採用している。-->現在「東京十社巡り」に荏原神社は入っていないが、品川神社、荏原神社ともに元准勅祭社を名乗っている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title= 品川神社【北の天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title= 荏原神社【天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。
[[1975年]]（[[昭和]]50年）、[[昭和天皇]]即位50年を奉祝して関係神社が協議を行い、准勅祭社から遠隔の府中町六所宮と埼玉県久喜市の鷲宮神社を外し、23区内の10社を巡る「東京十社巡り」が企画され、[[七福神]]巡りなどとともに<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=東京十社巡りガイド|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>観光的な要素を濃くして現在に至っている。
== 東京十社一覧 == [[式内社]]に列せられた神社はない。「近代」は明治に成立した[[近代社格制度]]の社格、「別表」は戦後の[[別表神社]]を示す。社名・鎮座地は現在のもの。 {|class="wikitable" !社名!!通称・別名!!主祭神!!近代!!別表!!鎮座地(東京都)!!備考 |- |[[根津神社]]||根津権現||[[須佐之男命]]、[[大山咋命]]、[[誉田別命]]||[[府社]]||||[[文京区]][[根津]]|| |- |[[芝大神宮]]||芝神明宮、飯倉神明宮||[[天照大神]]、[[豊受大神]]||府社||||[[港区 (東京都)|港区]][[芝大門]]|| |- |[[神田明神|神田神社]]||神田明神||[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[平将門|平将門命]]||府社||[[別表神社|別表]]||[[千代田区]][[外神田]]||<!--<ref>新・東京五社（他に、日枝神社、東京大神宮、[[水天宮]]、[[金刀比羅宮 (東京都港区)|金刀比羅宮]]）の一つ。</ref>--> |- |[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]||山王権現||[[大山咋神]]||[[官幣大社]]||別表||千代田区[[永田町]]||<ref group="注釈">東京五社<!--（他に、[[明治神宮]]、[[靖国神社]]、[[大國魂神社]]、[[東京大神宮]]）-->の一つ<!--、新・東京五社の一つ-->。</ref> |- |[[亀戸天神社]]||東宰府、亀戸天満宮||[[菅原道真|天満大神（菅原道真）]]、[[アメノホヒ|天菩日命]]||府社||||[[江東区]][[亀戸]]|| |- |[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]||||[[菊理姫命]]、[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]||[[郷社]]||||文京区[[白山 (文京区)|白山]]|| |- |[[品川神社]]||北の天王、北品川稲荷社||天比理乃咩命、[[素盞嗚尊]]、[[トヨウケビメ|宇賀之売命]]||郷社||||[[品川区]][[北品川]]||<ref group="注釈">[[東海七福神]]の[[大黒天|大黒]]</ref> |-
|- |[[富岡八幡宮]]||深川八幡||[[応神天皇|八幡神（応神天皇）]]||府社||||江東区[[富岡 (江東区)|富岡]]||<ref group="注釈">[[深川七福神]]の恵比寿</ref><ref group="注釈">戦後、別表神社となったが、2017年に神社本庁を離脱した。</ref> |- |[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]||王子権現||伊弉諾命、伊弉冉命、天照大御神||郷社||||[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[王子本町]]|| |- |[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]||||素盞嗚尊、[[奇稲田姫命]]、大己貴命||府社||||港区[[赤坂 (東京都港区)|赤坂]]|| |}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 外部リンク == * [http://10jinja.tokyo/ 東京十社めぐり]（東京十社会公式サイト）

'''東京十社'''（とうきょうじっしゃ）は、元准勅祭社12社の内から、[[1975年]]（[[昭和]]50年）に定められた東京近郊の10の[[神社]]のことである。
== 概要 == [[明治維新]]が成り[[東京奠都|東京に奠都]]した明治天皇は、[[1868年]]（明治元年）[[10月17日 (旧暦)|10月17日]]、[[氷川神社]]の例大祭を新たに勅祭に定めた。それに続いて[[11月8日 (旧暦)|11月8日]]、それまでの[[畿内]][[二十二社]]などの[[勅祭社]]に併せて東京近郊の主だった神社を'''准勅祭社'''（じゅんちょくさいしゃ）と定めて[[東京]]の鎮護と万民の安泰を祈る神社とした。
当初は12社（[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]・[[根津神社]]・[[芝大神宮|芝神明宮]]・[[神田明神|神田神社]]・[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]・[[亀戸天神社|亀戸神社]]・[[品川神社]]・[[富岡八幡宮|富岡八幡神社]]・[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]・[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]・[[大國魂神社|六所神社]]・[[鷲宮神社]]）であった。しかし早くも[[1870年]]（[[明治]]3年）[[9月1日 (旧暦)|9月1日]]には廃止され准勅祭社の制度は一時的なもので終わった。該当神社は府社あるいは郷社となる。第二次大戦後は政府による[[社格]]そのものが廃止された。なお、准勅祭社の品川神社については、<!-- 品川貴船社、現在の品川神社を稲荷神社としている例があること等から、この品川神社とは-->当時の品川貴船社、現在の[[荏原神社]]であるとする説もある<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=荏原神社-准勅祭社|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。<!--しかし品川神社第十三代神主小泉帯刀（准勅祭社指定当時の神主）の文書を根拠に現在の「東京十社めぐり」では品川神社説を採用している。-->現在「東京十社巡り」に荏原神社は入っていないが、品川神社、荏原神社ともに元准勅祭社を名乗っている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title= 品川神社【北の天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title= 荏原神社【天王社】|publisher=東京都神社庁|ref=harv|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>。
[[1975年]]（[[昭和]]50年）、[[昭和天皇]]即位50年を奉祝して関係神社が協議を行い、准勅祭社から遠隔の府中町六所宮と埼玉県久喜市の鷲宮神社を外し、23区内の10社を巡る「東京十社巡り」が企画され、[[七福神]]巡りなどとともに<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=東京十社巡りガイド|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>観光的な要素を濃くして現在に至っている。
== 東京十社一覧 == [[式内社]]に列せられた神社はない。「近代」は明治に成立した[[近代社格制度]]の社格、「別表」は戦後の[[別表神社]]を示す。社名・鎮座地は現在のもの。 {|class="wikitable" !社名!!通称・別名!!主祭神!!近代!!別表!!鎮座地(東京都)!!備考 |- |[[根津神社]]||根津権現||[[須佐之男命]]、[[大山咋命]]、[[誉田別命]]||[[府社]]||||[[文京区]][[根津]]|| |- |[[芝大神宮]]||芝神明宮、飯倉神明宮||[[天照大神]]、[[豊受大神]]||府社||||[[港区 (東京都)|港区]][[芝大門]]|| |- |[[神田明神|神田神社]]||神田明神||[[大己貴命]]、[[少彦名命]]、[[平将門|平将門命]]||府社||[[別表神社|別表]]||[[千代田区]][[外神田]]||<!--<ref>新・東京五社（他に、日枝神社、東京大神宮、[[水天宮]]、[[金刀比羅宮 (東京都港区)|金刀比羅宮]]）の一つ。</ref>--> |- |[[日枝神社 (千代田区)|日枝神社]]||山王権現||[[大山咋神]]||[[官幣大社]]||別表||千代田区[[永田町]]||<ref group="注釈">東京五社<!--（他に、[[明治神宮]]、[[靖国神社]]、[[大國魂神社]]、[[東京大神宮]]）-->の一つ<!--、新・東京五社の一つ-->。</ref> |- |[[亀戸天神社]]||東宰府、亀戸天満宮||[[菅原道真|天満大神（菅原道真）]]、[[アメノホヒ|天菩日命]]||府社||||[[江東区]][[亀戸]]|| |- |[[白山神社 (文京区)|白山神社]]||||[[菊理姫命]]、[[伊弉諾命]]、[[伊弉冊命]]||[[郷社]]||||文京区[[白山 (文京区)|白山]]|| |- |[[品川神社]]||北の天王、北品川稲荷社||天比理乃咩命、[[素盞嗚尊]]、[[トヨウケビメ|宇賀之売命]]||郷社||||[[品川区]][[北品川]]||<ref group="注釈">[[東海七福神]]の[[大黒天|大黒]]</ref> |-
|- |[[富岡八幡宮]]||深川八幡||[[応神天皇|八幡神（応神天皇）]]||府社||||江東区[[富岡 (江東区)|富岡]]||<ref group="注釈">[[深川七福神]]の恵比寿</ref><ref group="注釈">戦後、別表神社となったが、2017年に神社本庁を離脱した。</ref> |- |[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]||王子権現||伊弉諾命、伊弉冉命、天照大御神||郷社||||[[北区 (東京都)|北区]][[王子本町]]|| |- |[[氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|赤坂氷川神社]]||||素盞嗚尊、[[奇稲田姫命]]、大己貴命||府社||||港区[[赤坂 (東京都港区)|赤坂]]|| |}

{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}}
{{ill|Tokyo Ten Shrines|ja|東京十社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Ten Shrines|qq=}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|qq=}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|qq=}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|qq=}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|qq=}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|qq=}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|qq=}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|qq=}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |[[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]]||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|qq=}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|qq=}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |[[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]]||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|qq=}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|qq=}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |[[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]]||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukagawa Shichifukujin|qq=}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|qq=}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

{{ill|Tokyo Ten Shrines|ja|東京十社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Ten Shrines|qq=}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|qq=}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|qq=}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|qq=}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|qq=}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|qq=}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|qq=}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|qq=}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}}
{{ill|Tokyo Ten Shrines|ja|東京十社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Ten Shrines|qq=}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|qq=}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|qq=}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|qq=}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|qq=}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|qq=}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|qq=}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|qq=}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}}
{{ill|Tokyo Ten Shrines|ja|東京十社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Ten Shrines|qq=}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|qq=}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)]]
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|qq=}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|qq=}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|qq=}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|qq=}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|qq=}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|qq=}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|qq=}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|qq=}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|qq=}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |[[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]]||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukagawa Shichifukujin|qq=}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|qq=}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Akasaka|| |}

However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |[[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]]||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa
