* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>

* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]] and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of [[:en:Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)]] as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical [[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, [[:en:Nezu Shrine|Nezu Shrine]], {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, [[:en:Kameido Tenjin Shrine|Kameido Tenjin Shrine]], {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachimangu]], {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, [[:en:Ōkunitama Shrine|Okunitama Shrine]] (Rokusho Shrine), and [[:en:Washinomiya Shrine|Washinomiya Shrine]]. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after WWII.

* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|en|Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|WD=Q703633|lt=Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|en|Nezu Shrine|WD=Q335612|lt=Nezu Shrine}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|en|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|WD=Q11370953|lt=Kameido Tenjin Shrine}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|en|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|WD=Q654417|lt=Tomioka Hachimangu}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Ōkunitama Shrine|WD=Q611678|lt=Okunitama Shrine}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|Washinomiya Shrine|WD=Q611108|lt=Washinomiya Shrine}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine}} after WWII.

Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}}
{{ill|Tokyo Ten Shrines|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Ten Shrines|qq=}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo

* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]

Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|en|Nezu Shrine|WD=Q335612|lt=Nezu Shrine}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|en|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|WD=Q11370953|lt=Kameido Tenjin Shrine}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|en|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|WD=Q654417|lt=Tomioka Hachimangu}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Ōkunitama Shrine|WD=Q611678|lt=Okunitama Shrine}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|Washinomiya Shrine|WD=Q611108|lt=Washinomiya Shrine}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>

It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}}
{{ill|Tokyo Ten Shrines|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Ten Shrines|qq=}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|qq=}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|en|Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|WD=Q3135503|lt=Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)}}
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|en|Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|WD=Q703633|lt=Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|en|Nezu Shrine|WD=Q335612|lt=Nezu Shrine}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|en|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|WD=Q11370953|lt=Kameido Tenjin Shrine}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|en|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|WD=Q654417|lt=Tomioka Hachimangu}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Ōkunitama Shrine|WD=Q611678|lt=Okunitama Shrine}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|Washinomiya Shrine|WD=Q611108|lt=Washinomiya Shrine}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine}} after WWII.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Els 10 santuaris de Tòquio]][[de:Tōkyō-jissha]][[fr:Tokyo-jissha]][[ja:東京十社]][[zh:東京十社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Els 10 santuaris de Tòquio]][[de:Tōkyō-jissha]][[fr:Tokyo-jissha]][[ja:東京十社]][[zh:東京十社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Els 10 santuaris de Tòquio]][[de:Tōkyō-jissha]][[fr:Tokyo-jissha]][[ja:東京十社]][[zh:東京十社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:TokyoJisshaGroßesEmaBestückt.JPG|prefecture={{ill|ward area of Tokyo|WD=Q308891}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.65758828|N|139.75299239|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}
{{ill|Tokyo Ten Shrines|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Ten Shrines|qq=}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|qq=}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|en|Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|WD=Q3135503|lt=Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)}}
* [[Kameido Tenjin Shrine]] * [[Kanda Shrine]]
* [[Nezu Shrine]]
* [[Ōji Shrine]] * {{ill|Shiba Dai-jingū|pl|4=ja|5=芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Dai-jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Shinagawa-jinja|ja|品川神社|pl|Shinagawa-jinja|1=Shinagawa Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa-jinja|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}
* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|en|Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|WD=Q703633|lt=Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|en|Nezu Shrine|WD=Q335612|lt=Nezu Shrine}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|en|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|WD=Q11370953|lt=Kameido Tenjin Shrine}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|en|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|WD=Q654417|lt=Tomioka Hachimangu}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Ōkunitama Shrine|WD=Q611678|lt=Okunitama Shrine}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|Washinomiya Shrine|WD=Q611108|lt=Washinomiya Shrine}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine}} after WWII.
{|class="wikitable" !Shrine Name!!Common Name!!Main Deity!!Modern Rank!!Beppyo!!Location (Tokyo)!!Notes |- |{{ill|Nezu Shrine|en|Nezu Shrine|WD=Q335612|lt=Nezu Shrine}}||Nezu Gongen||Susanoo, Oyamakui, Hachiman||Fusha|| ||Bunkyo, Nezu|| |- |{{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Daijingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}||Shiba Shinmei-gu||Amaterasu, Toyouke-no-Okami||Fusha|| ||Minato, Shibadaimon|| |- |{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}||Kanda Myojin||Okuninushi, Sukunahikona, Taira no Masakado||Fusha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Sotokanda|| |- |{{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}||Sanno Gongen||Oyamakui-no-Kami||Kanpei Taisha||Beppyo||Chiyoda, Nagatacho||<ref group="note">One of the Tokyo Gossha (Five Major Shrines).</ref> |- |{{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|en|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|WD=Q11370953|lt=Kameido Tenjin Shrine}}||Kameido Tenmangu||Sugawara no Michizane, Ame-no-Hohi||Fusha|| ||Koto, Kameido|| |- |{{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}|| ||Kukurihime, Izanagi, Izanami||Gosha|| ||Bunkyo, Hakusan|| |- |{{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}||Kita-no-Tenno||Amenohirinome, Susanoo, Ukanomitama||Gosha|| ||Shinagawa, Kitashinagawa||<ref group="note">One of the {{ill|Tōkai Shichifukujin|ja|東海七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkai Shichifukujin|1=Tōkai Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28685055}} (Daikokuten).</ref> |- |{{ill|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|en|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|WD=Q654417|lt=Tomioka Hachimangu}}||Fukagawa Hachiman||Hachiman (Emperor Ojin)||Fusha|| ||Koto, Tomioka||<ref group="note">Ebisu of the {{ill|Fukagawa Shichifukujin|ja|深川七福神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukagawa Shichifukujin|1=Fukagawa Shichifukujin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11559993}}.</ref><ref group="note">Left the Jinja Honcho in 2017.</ref> |- |{{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}||Oji Gongen||Izanagi, Izanami, Amaterasu||Gosha|| ||Kita, Ojihoncho|| |- |{{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}|| ||Susanoo, Kushinadahime, Okuninushi||Fusha|| ||Minato, Akasaka|| |}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Els 10 santuaris de Tòquio]][[de:Tōkyō-jissha]][[fr:Tokyo-jissha]][[ja:東京十社]][[zh:東京十社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Els 10 santuaris de Tòquio]][[de:Tōkyō-jissha]][[fr:Tokyo-jissha]][[ja:東京十社]][[zh:東京十社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Els 10 santuaris de Tòquio]][[de:Tōkyō-jissha]][[fr:Tokyo-jissha]][[ja:東京十社]][[zh:東京十社]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:TokyoJisshaGroßesEmaBestückt.JPG|prefecture={{ill|ward area of Tokyo|WD=Q308891|1=Special wards of Tokyo}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.65758828|N|139.75299239|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}
{{ill|Tokyo Ten Shrines|ja|東京十社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Ten Shrines|qq=}} are a group of ten prominent shrines in tokyo
==List==
* {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|ja|白山神社 (文京区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo Ward)|qq=}} * [[Hie Shrine]] * {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|en|Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|WD=Q3135503|lt=Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|1=Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)}}

* [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]]
'''Tokyo Jissha''' (東京十社) refers to ten shrines located around Tokyo selected in 1975 from among the original twelve {{ill|Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja|准勅祭社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jun-Chokusai-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京十社|1=Tokyo Ten Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q164895}}.
== Overview == Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}} and {{ill|Tokyo Tento|ja|東京奠都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Tento|1=transfer of the capital to Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524722}}, Emperor Meiji decreed the annual festival of {{ill|Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|en|Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)|WD=Q703633|lt=Hikawa Shrine (Minato-ku, Tokyo)|1=Hikawa Shrine (Saitama)}} as a Chokusai (Imperial festival) on October 17, 1868. Subsequently, on November 8 of the same year, major shrines around Tokyo were designated as Jun-Chokusai-sha (Semi-Imperial Shrines), joining the historical {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} in Kinai as protectors of Tokyo and promoters of public welfare.
Originally, there were twelve Jun-Chokusai-sha: {{ill|Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|ja|日枝神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hie Shrine (Chiyoda)|1=Hie Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4189425}}, {{ill|Nezu Shrine|en|Nezu Shrine|WD=Q335612|lt=Nezu Shrine|1=Nezu Shrine}}, {{ill|Shiba Daijingu|ja|芝大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiba Daijingu|1=Shiba Dai-jingū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614619}}, {{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|神田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神田明神}}, {{ill|Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|ja|白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan Shrine (Bunkyo)|1=Hakusan Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11579587}}, {{ill|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|en|Kameido Tenjin Shrine|WD=Q11370953|lt=Kameido Tenjin Shrine|1=Kameido Tenjin Shrine}}, {{ill|Shinagawa Shrine|ja|品川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinagawa Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|en|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|WD=Q654417|lt=Tomioka Hachimangu|1=Tomioka Hachiman Shrine}}, {{ill|Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oji Shrine (Kita-ku, Tokyo)|1=Ōji Shrine (disambiguation)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573001}}, {{ill|Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja|赤坂氷川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akasaka Hikawa Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氷川神社 (東京都港区赤坂)|1=Hikawa Shrine (Akasaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3135503}}, {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Ōkunitama Shrine|WD=Q611678|lt=Okunitama Shrine|1=Ōkunitama Shrine}} (Rokusho Shrine), and {{ill|Washinomiya Shrine|en|Washinomiya Shrine|WD=Q611108|lt=Washinomiya Shrine|1=Washinomiya Shrine}}. However, the system was abolished on September 1, 1870, and these shrines were reclassified under different Ranks. Shrine Rankings themselves were abolished entirely after World War II. Notably, the original "Shinagawa Shrine" designation might have referred to the current {{ill|Ebara Shrine|ja|荏原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara Shrine|1=Ebara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11618312}}, previously known as Shinagawa Kifune Shrine.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ebarajinja.org/jyunchoku/index.html|title=Ebara Shrine - Jun-Chokusai-sha|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref> Currently, both Shinagawa Shrine and Ebara Shrine claim their historical status as Jun-Chokusai-sha, though only Shinagawa Shrine is included in the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri" pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/5264/|title=Shinagawa Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/shinagawa/200137/|title=Ebara Shrine|publisher=Tokyo Shrine Agency|accessdate=2021-1-6}}</ref>
In 1975, commemorating the 50th anniversary of Emperor Showa's accession, shrines agreed to exclude the distant Okunitama Shrine in Fuchu and Washinomiya Shrine in Kuki, Saitama, establishing the "Tokyo Jissha Meguri," which today remains popular among visitors alongside other tours like the Seven Lucky Gods pilgrimage.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.jinjya.com/sp/101215.php|title=Tokyo Jissha Meguri Guide|accessdate=2016-12-24}}</ref>
== List of Tokyo Jissha == None of these shrines are listed in the ancient {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} registry. "Modern" indicates Ranks in the Meiji-era shrine classification system, and "Beppyo" indicates shrines listed as {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} after WWII.
